meta.description: Modeltell - Pattern definitions for detecting linguistic fingerprints. Covers both lexical (word-level) and syntactic (grammatical construction) patterns.

syntactic_patterns.label: Tricolon (Rule of Three)
syntactic_patterns.description: Three-part lists, especially with ascending syllable count or dramatic escalation
syntactic_patterns.notes: LLMs massively overuse tricolons compared to human writers. Check for ascending complexity pattern.

syntactic_patterns.label: Whether-You're Pseudo-Inclusivity
syntactic_patterns.description: Opening with 'Whether you're X or Y' to fake audience inclusivity
examples: Whether you're a seasoned professional or just starting out
Whether you're a small business or a large enterprise
Whether you're looking to scale or optimize
syntactic_patterns.notes: Almost exclusively an LLM construction. Extremely rare in human-written copy.

syntactic_patterns.label: Hedging Opener
syntactic_patterns.description: Softening phrases that add no information
syntactic_patterns.notes: Filler construction. Human writers use occasionally; LLMs use constantly.

syntactic_patterns.label: Not-Just-But Escalation
examples: not just a tool, but a partner
not just about efficiency, but about transformation
doesn't just meet expectations - it exceeds them
syntactic_patterns.notes: Classic LLM upsell pattern. Frequency is the tell - humans use it sometimes, LLMs use it constantly.

syntactic_patterns.label: By-Gerund Instruction
syntactic_patterns.description: Starting sentences with 'By [verb]ing' as a pseudo-authoritative construction
syntactic_patterns.notes: Common in human writing too, but LLMs use it 3-5x more frequently.

syntactic_patterns.label: In-Today's-Landscape
examples: In an era of unprecedented change
syntactic_patterns.notes: One of the strongest LLM signals. Near-zero in quality human copywriting.

syntactic_patterns.label: Imagine-This Opening
syntactic_patterns.description: Opening with 'Imagine' to create forced engagement
syntactic_patterns.notes: Used by humans too (especially in talks), but LLMs default to it disproportionately.

syntactic_patterns.label: Em-Dash Dramatic Pivot
syntactic_patterns.description: Using em-dash for dramatic effect mid-sentence
examples: the result - a 73% reduction in incidents
and the best part - it's completely free
syntactic_patterns.notes: Em-dash frequency per word is a strong model discriminator. Some models use 5-10x human baseline.

syntactic_patterns.label: Power Verb Stacking
syntactic_patterns.description: Clustering action verbs for artificial energy
examples: drive growth, boost engagement, and accelerate revenue
empower teams to innovate and deliver
syntactic_patterns.notes: Count density of power verbs per 100 words. LLMs cluster them; humans spread them out.

syntactic_patterns.label: Let's-Dive Transition
syntactic_patterns.description: Conversational transitions that signal section breaks
syntactic_patterns.notes: Section transition filler. Human writers vary transitions more.

syntactic_patterns.label: Colon-Introduced List
syntactic_patterns.description: Setting up bullet points with a colon clause
examples: Our approach is built on three pillars:
syntactic_patterns.notes: Structural pattern. Compare colon-to-list ratio between models.

syntactic_patterns.label: Rhetorical Question Hook
syntactic_patterns.description: Using questions as engagement bait, especially at the start
examples: What if there was a better way?
But what does this mean for your business?
syntactic_patterns.notes: Track position - LLMs cluster these at section starts. Humans distribute more evenly.

syntactic_patterns.label: Future-Forward Closing
examples: The future of X is here
syntactic_patterns.notes: Meaningless closing filler. Strong LLM signal especially in business content.

syntactic_patterns.label: Parenthetical Aside
syntactic_patterns.notes: Track frequency. Some models over-use parentheticals for false intimacy.

syntactic_patterns.label: At-Its-Core Pretension
syntactic_patterns.description: Pseudo-depth construction claiming to reveal essence
examples: When you strip it all away
syntactic_patterns.notes: Models use this to signal depth without providing it.

lexical_watchlist.description: Words to track the frequency of. These are known LLM-associated terms. NOTE: the pipeline measures their frequency and cross-model distinctiveness (TF-IDF vs the aggregate of all models), NOT overuse versus a human baseline - a matched human corpus is not yet implemented.
filler_phrases: at the end of the day

analysis_config.notes: FUTURE WORK (not implemented): compute TF-IDF against a matched human-written baseline corpus (e.g. published marketing copy, real press releases, human blog posts) to claim 'overuse vs humans'. Today the baseline is the aggregate of all models, so scores express cross-model distinctiveness only.
